title: Digital Timing Diagrams
description: Read and interpret WaveDrom timing diagrams. Understand clock edges, setup/hold times, and signal relationships.

simulatorProps.title: D Flip-Flop timing
simulatorProps.source: {signal: [{name:'CLK', wave:'P.......'},{name:'D',   wave:'x.1..0.x'},{name:'Q',   wave:'x...1.0x'},{},  {name:'Setup', wave:'xx.=..xx', data:['tsu']},{name:'Hold',  wave:'xxx=.xxx', data:['th']}]}

steps.instruction: A timing diagram shows signal states over time. The horizontal axis is time; each vertical division is one clock period. 'P' in WaveDrom means a periodic clock (high/low squares).
steps.hint: CLK is the master clock. All other signals are measured relative to its rising (↑) and falling (↓) edges.

steps.instruction: D is the data input to a flip-flop. 'x' means don't-care / unknown. '1' and '0' are logic levels. Observe how D transitions between these states.
steps.hint: In synchronous digital design, D must be stable for a setup time (tsu) before and a hold time (th) after the clock edge.

steps.instruction: Q is the output. Notice that Q only changes after the rising clock edge — the flip-flop samples D at that instant and holds the value until the next edge.
steps.hint: This is the core concept of synchronous logic: outputs change only on clock edges, preventing glitches from propagating.

steps.instruction: The 'Setup' row marks the setup window — D must be stable for this duration before the clock edge. Violating setup time causes a metastability failure.
steps.hint: Metastability means the flip-flop output is neither 0 nor 1 and can oscillate unpredictably until it resolves.

steps.instruction: The 'Hold' row marks the hold time after the clock edge. D must remain stable for this window as well. Hold violations occur in very fast paths (e.g. zero-delay lines).
steps.hint: Setup time limits the maximum clock frequency; hold time is fixed by the circuit and cannot be fixed by slowing the clock.
